title: Cleavers spring infusion
subtitle: A lymphatic tonic for early spring
excerpt: Fresh or dried cleavers herb, cold-steeped in water. A traditional spring lymphatic tonic used in European folk medicine to support lymphatic flow after winter stagnation.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, cleavers entry: the lymphatic and diuretic tradition, the spring tonic use. Nicholas Culpeper (the 17th-century herbalist), The English Physician, cleavers entry, the cooling and cleansing tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cold-steep overnight in the fridge for morning use. Fresh cleavers are available March-May; dried cleavers work year-round.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally used to support the lymphatic system. In western herbal medicine, lymphatic herbs are used when lymph nodes feel swollen and the reader feels generally sluggish and stagnant after illness or winter.

content.text: Cleavers (Galium aparine, the sticky weed that clings to clothing) is the western herbal tradition's primary spring

herb. Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer) records it as a traditional spring tonic, diuretic, and lymphatic herb in British folk medicine. The preparation is a cold infusion (room temperature or fridge, 1-8 hours), because hot water destroys some of the active active compounds. Fresh cleavers, cold-steeped overnight, give the most potent preparation; dried cleavers work year-round. The taste is mild and green, easy to drink.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Diuretics and lithium. Cleavers has a mild diuretic action. Readers on diuretic medication or lithium should check with their prescriber before regular use. The combined effect can change electrolyte balance and drug levels.

Swollen lymph nodes. Persistent, firm, or growing lymph nodes in the neck, armpits, or groin need medical investigation. This preparation is for general spring sluggishness, not for significant lymph node problems.

items.prepNote: dried cleavers aerial parts, about 1 heaped teaspoon; or a large handful of fresh, rinsed cleavers
cold or room-temperature water; do not use hot water for this preparation

content.text: Combine the cleavers and cold water in a jug or covered glass. Leave to steep at room temperature for 1 hour, or overnight in the fridge for a morning dose. Strain through a sieve. Drink at room temperature or gently warmed (do not boil). The infusion should be pale green and mildly herbal in taste.

One cup (250 ml) once or twice a day, for 2-4 weeks in spring. The traditional spring tonic course is 3-4 weeks at the first appearance of fresh cleavers in the garden (approximately March-May in the UK). Take at least 2 hours away from prescription medication. Paediatric use: not for children under twelve without specialist advice.
The cold infusion keeps 24 hours in the fridge in a sealed jar. Dried cleavers stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy
On diuretics or lithium without prescriber approval. Persistent, enlarged, or firm lymph nodes need medical investigation.
